quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Le Chatelier's Principle"?

options.text: The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Le Chatelier's Principle" is defined as: When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Concentration change"?

options.text: Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it
An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally
Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Concentration change" is defined as: Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Pressure/volume change"?

options.text: The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
The number of shared electron pairs between two atoms: single (1), double (2), triple (3); higher order = shorter, stronger bond
The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect

quiz_questions.explanation: "Pressure/volume change" is defined as: Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Temperature change"?

options.text: An acid that can donate more than one proton: diprotic (H₂SO₄, H₂CO₃) or triprotic (H₃PO₄)
Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area
A mixture of two or more metals (or a metal and nonmetal); substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms, interstitial alloys have small atoms in gaps
The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Temperature change" is defined as: The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Catalyst effect"?

options.text: π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules
A catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching equilibrium faster but NOT changing its position or K
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system

quiz_questions.explanation: "Catalyst effect" is defined as: A catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching equilibrium faster but NOT changing its position or K. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "When a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts in the direction that partially counteracts the disturbance"?

options.text: Buffer capacity
Stoichiometric conversion
Rate law
Le Chatelier's Principle

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Le Chatelier's Principle".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Adding a species shifts equilibrium away from it; removing a species shifts equilibrium toward it"?

options.text: Lewis acid/base
Spectator ion
Spontaneous process
Concentration change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Concentration change".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas; inert gas at constant V has no effect"?

options.text: Pressure/volume change
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Equilibrium vs. completion
ICE table

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Pressure/volume change".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction"?

options.text: Dipole-dipole interactions
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Metallic bond
Temperature change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Temperature change".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A catalyst speeds up both forward and reverse reactions equally, reaching equilibrium faster but NOT changing its position or K"?

options.text: Catalyst effect
Standard free energy of formation
Homogeneous catalyst
Polarizability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Catalyst effect".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Le Chatelier's Principle is accurate?

options.text: Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
Strong acids (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃) and strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka or Kb values.
The atomic number (Z) equals the number of protons and defines the element.
Le Chatelier's Principle predicts how an equilibrium system responds to disturbances: the system shifts to partially counteract the change.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Le Chatelier's Principle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Adding reactant or removing product shifts equilibrium forward (toward products); adding product or removing reactant shifts it in reverse.
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.
Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Le Chatelier's Principle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.

Acid-Base Definitions have evolved from Arrhenius (acids produce H⁺, bases produce OH⁻ in water) to Brønsted-Lowry (acids donate H⁺, bases accept H⁺) to Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs).
Balancing Chemical Equations ensures the law of conservation of mass is satisfied: the same number of each type of atom appears on both sides.
Temperature determines spontaneity when ΔH and ΔS have the same sign: ΔH  0 → always spontaneous; ΔH > 0 and ΔS < 0 → never spontaneous.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Le Chatelier's Principle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
A valid mechanism must: (1) have elementary steps that sum to the overall equation, (2) be consistent with the experimentally determined rate law, and (3) have each step be physically reasonable.
Temperature changes alter K: increasing T shifts endothermic reactions forward (K increases) and exothermic reactions in reverse (K decreases).
Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Le Chatelier's Principle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Weak acids/bases partially dissociate with Ka/Kb values <1.
Phase changes (melting, boiling, sublimation, condensation, freezing, deposition) occur at specific temperatures where energy input/removal overcomes or establishes IMFs.
Catalysts do NOT shift equilibrium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Le Chatelier's Principle as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Haber process optimization: high pressure (fewer gas moles on product side, Δn = -2) and low temperature (exothermic) favor NH₃, but moderate T used for acceptable rate
Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt
Stoichiometric calculations in pharmaceutical manufacturing ensuring precise drug composition, maintaining consistent dosage and efficacy
Baking chemistry: using the correct stoichiometric ratio of baking soda (NaHCO₃) to acid (e.g., cream of tartar) to produce the right amount of CO₂ for leavening

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old
Breathing regulation: increased CO₂ in blood shifts CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ rightward, lowering pH and signaling the brain to increase breathing rate

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Le Chatelier's Principle applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Industrial removal of products: in esterification (alcohol + acid ⇌ ester + water), removing water with molecular sieves shifts equilibrium toward ester, increasing yield
Household acids and bases: vinegar (acetic acid, Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵), lemon juice (citric acid), baking soda (NaHCO₃ acts as a weak base)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Le Chatelier's Principle?

options.text: Haber process optimization: high pressure (fewer gas moles on product side, Δn = -2) and low temperature (exothermic) favor NH₃, but moderate T used for acceptable rate
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Hemoglobin-oxygen binding equilibrium shifting in lungs (high O₂, favoring HbO₂) vs. tissues (low O₂, favoring O₂ release)
SN1 and SN2 mechanisms in organic chemistry: SN1 (two-step, carbocation intermediate) vs. SN2 (one-step, bimolecular) determine reaction outcomes in drug synthesis

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Le Chatelier's Principle in practice?

options.text: Radioactive decay following first-order kinetics with constant half-life: carbon-14 (t₁/₂ = 5,730 years) used for archaeological dating
Electroplating using electrolysis: depositing a thin layer of metal (chrome, gold, nickel) onto objects for corrosion protection or aesthetics
Breathing regulation: increased CO₂ in blood shifts CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ rightward, lowering pH and signaling the brain to increase breathing rate
Rocket fuel calculations: precise stoichiometry of liquid H₂ and O₂ (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O) ensures maximum thrust without wasting expensive fuel

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Le Chatelier's Principle.
